text: This is Logan Mellert with National Surgery Review. Thank you for joining us today as we walk you through a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon accesses the abdomen at Palmer's point in the left upper quadrant using an optical trocar. A 5mm trocar is inserted at the umbilicus, and two 3mm trocars are positioned along the right costal margin.

Atraumatic graspers are used to grasp and retract the gallbladder cephalad and laterally, exposing the triangle of Calot. The initial inspection reveals the cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct.
The surgeon begins careful dissection using an L-hook electrocautery, hooking adhesions and applying anterior tension to avoid injury to the duodenum or ductal structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes perineal attachments behind the gallbladder, pausing to identify the surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon dissects within the triangle of Calot, defined by the common hepatic duct medially, the cystic duct laterally, and the inferior edge of the liver superiorly. The cystic artery is identified on the gallbladder wall, guiding further dissection in this three-dimensional space.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully dissect the cystic duct, removing attachments from both the anterior and posterior aspects to clearly delineate the duct from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon focuses on the cystic artery after fully dissecting the cystic duct. The critical view of safety is achieved by clearing the hepatocystic triangle of fat and fibrinous tissues, revealing only the cystic duct and artery entering the gallbladder, with the lower third of the cystic plate exposed.

The surgeon attempts to identify the common bile duct and common hepatic duct before ligating the cystic duct, ensuring clarity of anatomical structures. If anatomical ambiguity is present, a cholangiogram may be performed to visually delineate these structures.
The surgeon confirms the anatomy and uses plastic clips to ligate the cystic duct, placing two clips proximally and one clip distally.
The surgeon uses hook scissors to divide the cystic duct, opting for this instrument to minimize the risk of inadvertent injury during the procedure.

The surgeon places two clips on the cystic artery and uses electrocautery to divide it distally. A small posterior cystic artery branch is identified and managed similarly, with one clip placed proximally and electrocautery used for distal division.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed using electrocautery, carefully maintaining the areolar plane to avoid liver bed bleeding or gallbladder entry. Liver bed bleeding is promptly controlled with electrocautery. In cases of acute cholecystitis, the plane is more clearly defined due to tissue edema.

The surgeon grasps the gallbladder at the cystic duct and pulls it through the trocar, removing it from the abdomen. In cases without cholelithiasis or significant pathology, the gallbladder is extracted without a retrieval bag.
The surgeon reinspects the liver bed for hemostasis following the removal of the gallbladder, ensuring no bleeding is present before desufflation.
